content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 30, 2018

Brian Oneto
Supervisor 5th District
Amador County
810 Court Street
Jackson, CA 95642

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-033

Dear Mr. Oneto:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act and Section 1090, and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when considering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Amador County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

May you bid on a tree removal project for the Amador Water Agency given that you are an Amador County Supervisor?

CONCLUSION

Yes. You may submit a bid to perform tree removal services for Amador Water Agency because you are acting in your private capacity and not as a County Supervisor under the Act and Section 1090.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-033
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You are an Amador County Supervisor. You would like to submit a bid on a tree removal project, which will be put out for bid by Amador Water Agency (the "AWA"). AWA and Amador County are distinct entities with separate boards of directors.

At a Board of Supervisors meeting on February 13, 2018, AWA requested allocation of a portion of the County's CalFire grant, which covers 25% of the costs for tree mortality operations. You recused yourself from participating in the Board's decision to allocate the grant funds and left the meeting room. The remaining Supervisors decided to allocate $19,000 of the County's grant award to AWA for hazard tree removal, after identifying that AWA was eligible to receive the funds.

ANALYSIS

Political Reform Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Specific to your facts, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise, including:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.

Under your facts, you have identified potential interests in your tree service business and a source of income interest from the business, assuming you meet the applicable financial standards.²

You stated that you recused yourself from making or participating in the Board's decision to allocate grant funds to AWA and left the room. Thus, the upcoming decision concerns AWA's bid selection for performance of the tree removal project. You stated that AWA and the County are

_______________________
² An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family members is not considered separately if the decision also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Because the decision may affect your business interest, we do not analyze your interest in your personal finances.

File No. A-18-033
Page No. 3

separate and distinct entities with independent governing boards. Therefore, it does not appear that
AWA is subject to the Board's authority or approval. As both agencies appear to be separate
entities, the Board would not be making or participating in making AWA's decision to select an
appropriate bid to perform the tree removal services. As a result, the remaining question is whether
submitting a bid to AWA to perform tree removal services arises to "using your position to
influence the decision."

Influencing a Governmental Decision

AWA and the County are separate and distinct entities with independent governing boards,
we therefore assume AWA is not subject to the authority or budgetary control of the County.
Pertinent to your facts, Regulation 18704(c)(2) provides that an official "attempts to use his or her
official position to influence a decision" if "the official contacts or appears before any official in
any other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and … acts or purports to act
within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact."

By submitting a bid to perform tree removal services for AWA, you would not be acting in
your official capacity as a County Supervisor, but in your private capacity on behalf of your
business. Therefore, so long as you do not act or purport to act on behalf of, or as a representative
of, the County, you would not be prohibited from submitting a bid to perform tree removal services
for AWA.³

Section 1090

Generally, Section 1090 prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they have a financial interest. Under Section 1090, "the prohibited
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) The purpose of Section 1090 is to make certain that "every public
officer be guided solely by the public interest, rather than by personal interest, when dealing with
contracts in an official capacity." (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 650.) Section 1090
applies to officials who participate in any way in the making of the contract, including involvement
in matters such as preliminary discussions, negotiations, planning, drawing of plans and
specifications. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d
222, 237.) Participation in the making of a contract is defined very broadly. A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void, and the prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are
fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 646-649.)

Generally, Section 1090 prohibits the Board from making a contract in which a member of
the Board has a financial interest. In this case, the County is not a party to the contract, nor does the
County appear to have authority over AWA's contracts, and does not appear to provide input for
contracts entered into by AWA. Even assuming AWA's appearance before the Board seeking grant
funds amounted to "input" on the contract, you recused yourself from participating in that decision.
Therefore, Section 1090 would not prohibit you from making, participating in the making of, or
entering into a contract with AWA in your private capacity.

³ For example, none of your written communications with AWA may use stationery with the County's
letterhead or sent from your County e-mail address.

File No. A-18-033
Page No. 4

Accordingly, you are permitted to submit a bid to AWA to perform the tree removal services in your private capacity under the Act and Section 1090.4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[Signature]

By: Sara K. Puricelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:jgl

___________________________
4 Upon entering into a contract with AWA, you may develop a disqualifying conflict of interest, which would prohibit you from taking part in future decisions or contracts with AWA. Additionally, Section 1090 may preclude future County allocations of grant funds to AWA if you have a contract with AWA at the time of the allocation. If you enter into a contract to perform the tree removal project, you may wish to seek further advice if a future decision or contract involving AWA will be considered.
